original_description: Run ad-hoc SQL against Mozilla telemetry tables using the bq CLI. Use when the task needs raw BigQuery access — dry-run cost checks, custom SQL, pulling DAU/MAU/Windows-distribution/Glean-metric data into a script. DO NOT USE FOR saved/shared queries with visualizations (use redash) or finding which probe to query (use mozdata:probe-discovery).
best_description: Run ad-hoc SQL against Mozilla telemetry tables using the bq CLI. Use when the task needs raw BigQuery access — dry-run cost checks, custom SQL, pulling DAU/MAU/Windows-distribution/Glean-metric data into a script. DO NOT USE FOR saved/shared queries with visualizations (use redash) or finding which probe to query (use mozdata:probe-discovery).
final_description: Run BigQuery SQL against Mozilla telemetry from the terminal. Use whenever the user wants query results — running a SELECT, doing a dry-run for scan cost, counting clients/DAU/MAU, pulling OS/Windows/macOS/Linux version distributions, extracting Glean metrics, or deciding which telemetry table to actually query for a count. Triggers on `bq`, `mozdata`, `moz-fx-data-shared-prod`, `firefox_desktop.*`, `baseline_clients_daily`, `baseline_clients_last_seen`, `active_users_aggregates_v3`, `events_stream`, partition filters, `sample_id`, or any pasted SQL the user expects executed. Also fires for "should I use table X or Y?" and cross-namespace join questions when execution is implied. Skip for saved/visualized/shared queries (use `redash`) or finding a probe with no execution intent (use `mozdata:probe-discovery`).

history.description: Run ad-hoc SQL against Mozilla telemetry tables using the bq CLI. Use when the task needs raw BigQuery access — dry-run cost checks, custom SQL, pulling DAU/MAU/Windows-distribution/Glean-metric data into a script. DO NOT USE FOR saved/shared queries with visualizations (use redash) or finding which probe to query (use mozdata:probe-discovery).

train_results.query: I need to write SQL that joins desktop and mobile telemetry — but they're separate namespaces, right? Show me the right approach.
Use bq to grab the top 10 macOS versions by client count — make sure to include the partition filter and use sample_id = 0.
What's the cheapest way to count DAU yesterday — should I use baseline_clients_daily or active_users_aggregates_v3? Use bq.
Run this against mozdata: `SELECT os, COUNT(DISTINCT client_id) FROM firefox_desktop.baseline_clients_daily WHERE submission_date = '2026-04-15' GROUP BY os` — output as a table.
Can you do a dry-run on this and tell me how many bytes it'll scan? `SELECT * FROM moz-fx-data-shared-prod.firefox_desktop.baseline_clients_daily WHERE submission_date = '2026-04-15'`
Use bq to count clients on Windows 11 versions yesterday — sample_id 0 is fine for a quick estimate.
Why does the Airflow DAG `bqetl_events_stream` keep failing? Check the logs.
Find the Glean probe that measures memory usage on Firefox Desktop — don't know the metric name.
Search Bugzilla for crash bugs filed in the last week — need to triage them this morning.
Pull this saved Redash query and chart it — query ID is 65967, and I want to share the result in an email.
Generate a daily log of my Claude Code and Codex sessions today.
Spin up a debug VM matching the win11-64-24h2 worker config — I need the same vmSize as production workers use.

test_results.query: BigQuery is giving me 'Access Denied'. I'm authed as jmoss@mozilla.com. What's the issue and how do I fix it?
Custom BigQuery: clients_last_seen MAU broken down by os_version_major for Firefox Desktop, last 28 days.
Pull events_stream rows for `browser.engagement.tab_open` over the last 3 days into a JSON file at ~/moz_artifacts/tab_open.json
Write a query against firefox_desktop_derived.active_users_aggregates_v3 for DAU by country for last week — pretty format.
Run a search in Splunk for VM provisioning failures yesterday on win11-64-25h2 RG.
Investigate failing task fuCPrKG2T62-4YH1tWYa7Q — image regression after the win11-25h2 bump?
Which tasks run on win11-64-25h2 — I need to know for the migration off 24h2.
Compare Windows 11 file versions between KB5070881 and KB5072359 — looking at ntdll.dll and tcpip.sys.

history.description: Execute SQL against Mozilla telemetry from the command line via the `bq` CLI — actually running the query, not just writing one. Triggers when the user wants results in the terminal: dry-runs to estimate scan cost, ad-hoc counts of clients/DAU/MAU, OS or Windows/macOS/Linux version distributions, Glean metric pulls, joins across telemetry tables, sample_id checks, partition-filter cost optimization, or piping query output into a script. Mentions of "bq", "BigQuery CLI", "run this query", "dry-run", `moz-fx-data-shared-prod`, `baseline_clients_daily`, `events_stream`, or `active_users_aggregates_v3` are strong signals. Skip for: saved/shared queries with charts (use redash), finding which probe to instrument or query (use mozdata:probe-discovery), or pure query-authoring guidance without execution (use mozdata:query-writing).

history.description: Use to execute BigQuery SQL against Mozilla telemetry from the terminal via the `bq` CLI. This is the right skill whenever the user wants query results in the shell, not a web chart — running SELECTs, dry-runs to estimate scan bytes, counting clients/DAU/MAU, pulling OS or Windows/macOS/Linux version breakdowns, Glean metric extracts, joining telemetry tables, sample_id sanity checks, or piping output into a script. Strong signals: "bq", "use bq", "run this query", "dry-run", "mozdata", "shared-prod", `baseline_clients_daily`, `active_users_aggregates_v3`, `events_stream`, or any pasted SQL the user wants executed. Also fires when the user asks which Mozilla telemetry table is cheapest/right for a count and expects you to actually run it. Skip when: results need to be saved/visualized/shared (use redash), the user only wants to find a probe (use mozdata:probe-discovery), or the user wants query authoring help with no execution intent (use mozdata:query-writing).

history.description: BigQuery SQL execution via the `bq` CLI. ACTUAL execution — running, dry-running, counting, pulling rows — not just authoring or charting. ALWAYS USE WHEN: user types "bq", "use bq", "run this", "run this query", "dry-run"; pastes a SELECT against `mozdata`/`moz-fx-data-shared-prod`/`firefox_desktop.*`/`telemetry.*`; asks for terminal-output counts of clients/DAU/MAU; pulls OS or Windows/macOS/Linux/Darwin/kernel version distributions; mentions partition filter, `sample_id`, `baseline_clients_daily`, `baseline_clients_last_seen`, `active_users_aggregates_v3`, `events_stream`, or Glean metric extracts; asks which Mozilla telemetry table is cheapest/right for a count; or wants joins across telemetry namespaces actually run. Beats `mozdata:query-writing` whenever the user expects a real result back — including "should I use table X or Y?" framings. Skip only when the user wants saved/visualized/shared queries (use `redash`), or just needs to find a probe with no execution intent (use `mozdata:probe-discovery`).

Use bq to count clients on Windows 11 versions yesterday — sample_id 0 is fine for a quick estimate.
